trajectory_summary: The call is a logistics confirmation with a pre-sold customer. Gary enters warm, self-resolves every potential objection, and books without Joel having to earn the appointment. Energy is nominally positive throughout, but the shape is stagnant from a qualification standpoint — no verify items completed, no bill anchor, no co-owner check, no button-up protocol. The appointment is booked on Gary's goodwill alone.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment, which v1 would likely tag as a positive outcome. That tag is technically accurate but misleading. The appointment is structurally fragile: no qualification data was gathered, no co-owner on title was confirmed, no bill was collected (lead type unknown — if paid lead, this is a protocol failure), and no button-up elements (computer, no-driving, 'is that fair' handshake) were completed. The booking exists because Gary is self-motivated, not because Joel built a qualified sit. Show-rate risk is real.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up protocol and verify completion on reschedule calls
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Awesome. No problem. Yeah. So I'll go ahead and get you on our calendar here for tomorrow at 9AM then. Yeah. I'll leave you a text as well.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Joel treated this as a pure logistics call and booked without completing a single verify item or button-up element. On a next-morning appointment with a customer who explicitly said 'six months, I don't care,' the missing 'is that fair' handshake and computer/no-driving anchor are the two highest-leverage show-rate protectors Joel left on the table. The manager should walk Joel through the button-up checklist against this transcript line by line — every required element is absent — and establish that reschedule calls carry the same protocol requirements as first-touch calls.

key_moments.quote: Well, that's kinda why most people are looking into it is money.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's only bill-anchor opportunity and Joel fumbles it twice simultaneously. First, he delivers a generic group statement ('most people') instead of asking Gary about his specific bill. Second, the timing is reactive — Gary has just said 'especially if you're just trying to save money' and Joel echoes the category rather than drilling into Gary's number. The schema requires capture AND flip (ask + acknowledge + flip); Joel does neither. The generic framing directly triggers Gary's age-based savings objection at 04:20 ('I'm 82, what am I gonna save it for?') — a predictable consequence of not personalizing the anchor.

key_moments.quote: the problem with saving money, I'm 82, and what am I gonna save it for?
key_moments.stewart_read: Gary is disclosing age-based existential ambivalence about the value of saving money — this is simultaneously a vulnerability disclosure and an objection_inversion opportunity. Joel says nothing. Gary answers his own objection at 04:27 ('it's just the principle of it all'). The miss is double: Joel neither acknowledges the human weight of the statement nor deploys the inversion. The schema's inversion framework applies directly here — the flip is not about savings, it's about not wasting money on a bill that doesn't have to exist. Gary even hands Joel the language at 04:33 ('not wasting your money').

key_moments.quote: I you know, I I want it on the on my outbuildings, you know, and, that type of thing.
key_moments.stewart_read: Gary is describing a multi-structure solar installation — outbuildings plus the main house, with a centralized electrical distribution point and space for batteries. This is a higher-complexity, higher-value lead than a standard single-home install. Joel does not capture any of this detail, does not ask about the outbuilding square footage or usage, and does not flag the battery interest for the closer. The enthusiasm signal is rich: Gary has thought about the system architecture, knows where the transformer is, and has already discussed the setup with a prior solar rep.

key_moments.quote: I can do it next month, six months. I don't care. You know? It's no big deal.
key_moments.stewart_read: Gary is explicitly signaling zero urgency — 'six months, I don't care.' This is a fragility marker for the appointment booked the next morning. Joel does not respond to it at all. The schema's inversion framework applies: the flip here is not about installation timeline but about the bill Gary is paying every month while he waits. Gary has already told Joel he cares about 'not wasting money' (04:33) — that's the inversion lever. The flip doesn't require fabricated numbers; it requires pointing Gary's own stated value ('not wasting money') at the cost of delay. Without the flip, the no-urgency framing is left standing, which increases no-show risk on a next-morning appointment.
